Just now

--

How to Use AI to Actually Get Smarter (Without Pretending You Already Are)
My friend said something a few months back that just stuck with me.
“Man, I wish I was smarter.”
And look, he’s not lazy. He does decent work. But he genuinely believed that smart people just… come into the world with bigger brains or better memory or something he didn’t get.
So I asked him, “How much time did you actually spend learning something new this week?”
He paused. Then kind of laughed.
“Honestly? Like… barely any.”
And that’s when it hit me.
Nobody’s actually lacking brains. What most people lack is a way to learn that doesn’t feel like pulling teeth.
That’s where AI is actually changing things.
Not because it makes you magically smarter. But because it gets rid of all the friction that makes learning slow, confusing, and boring as hell.
— -
**The biggest lie people believe about AI**
Everyone thinks using ChatGPT means letting the AI do all the work while you chill.
That’s literally the worst way to use it.
The people I know who actually got smarter? They don’t let AI think for them.
They use it to think *better*.
— -
**Here’s what I mean**
Say you’re trying to understand how investing works.
Normally? You’re bouncing between random articles, YouTube videos, everyone contradicting everyone else. Takes forever. Confuses everything.
Or you could ask AI to explain investing like you’re a twelve-year-old.
Then ask it to explain the same thing like a university professor.
Ask for real examples.
Ask it to quiz you until you actually get it.
Suddenly learning isn’t passive. It’s interactive. Completely different.
— -
**I watched someone do this right**
This university student was prepping for exams.
Not cheating, though. She asked AI to generate practice questions. Explain hard concepts in simpler language. Summarize those massive chapters. Tell her what she was actually weak at.
She wasn’t letting AI answer for her.
She was learning faster.
— -
**This works everywhere, by the way**
Business owners? Using it to understand marketing without spending thousands on courses.
Freelancers? Better proposals.
Programmers? Understanding code they’ve never seen before.
Writers? Organizing thoughts that are all over the place.
Designers? Getting ideas to flow.
AI isn’t replacing the actual work. It’s making the path to being good at something way shorter.
— -
**Here’s the thing about asking good questions**
Most people ask AI something like, “Teach me digital marketing.”
And then wonder why the answer feels generic.
Try this instead: “I’m completely new to this. No marketing experience at all. Give me a 30-day plan with actual exercises and explain everything using real business examples — not theory.”
Suddenly the answer changes.
Specificity is everything with AI.
— -
**Different parts of the world, different reasons for using this**
In Pakistan? A lot of students use AI because private tutors cost money they don’t have. Expensive courses aren’t accessible. AI fills that gap.
In the US, UK, Canada? It’s more about professionals using it to speed up research, writing, coding, thinking through problems. It’s a productivity thing.
Different reasons. Same benefit.
Learn faster.
— -
**Something people don’t realize: AI gets things wrong sometimes**
Like, it’ll confidently tell you something that’s half-true or completely made up.
So don’t just accept everything it says.
Question it. Actually verify important stuff. Look at different angles.
Weirdly, AI rewards curious people way more than people who just passively accept answers.
If you ask one question and move on? You learn almost nothing.
But if you keep asking “Why though?”, “Can you say that differently?”, “Show me a real example,” “How do I actually use this?” — your brain actually absorbs it.
That’s literally what a good teacher does.
They don’t just hand you answers. They make you curious and guide you toward understanding.
— -
**A habit that actually works**
I call it the “AI Learning Loop.”
Pick a topic.
Ask AI to explain it simply.
Ask for examples.
Ask what people usually mess up with this.
Ask it to test if you understand it.
Then — and this is important — explain it back in your own words.
If you can’t explain it clearly? You haven’t really learned it.
That habit alone beats watching random videos for hours.
— -
**Where this is heading**
The world isn’t gonna need people who memorized the most facts. Facts are everywhere. Anyone can Google.
It’s gonna need people who ask better questions. Who connect ideas. Who actually solve problems. Who never stop learning.
AI can help with all of that.
But it can’t replace your curiosity.
That part’s on you.
— -
**If you actually want to get smarter**
Don’t go looking for the fanciest AI tool.
First, get better at learning itself.
Then use AI as your tutor. Your coach. Your debate partner. Your practice interviewer. Your editor. Your research assistant.
The people who win with AI? They’re not the ones who let it do the thinking.
They’re the ones who use it to think better.
And that skill stays valuable no matter how crazy AI gets.
